coredata.dc:title: In-situ determination of ferric ions in human blood serum using highly fluorescence nitrogen and boron co-doped carbon dots nanoprobe without serum preparation step: A direct and solvent-free method
coredata.dc:creator: Jafari, Seyed Mostafa
Masoum, Saeed
coredata.dc:description: Different methods have been reported for Fe3+ ions determination as an essential element in many physiological processes, but most of these methods suffer from the consumption of solvent and time in the blood serum sample preparation stage. In this study, we report fabrication of a selective and sensitive high fluorescent nanoprobe based on the boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots for Fe3+ determination in aqueous solution and human blood serum as an important biological sample. Interestingly, under mildly acidic conditions, adjusted ionic strength, and the existence of EDTA, Fe3+ ions can be released from human serum transferrin, with no need for serum protein removal process as a separate step in sample preparation. Indeed, based on the innovative approach, both steps (consisting of Fe3+ ion release of protein and its detection) are in-situ performed. Notably, limit of detection of Fe3+ was improved from 4.72 μM to 0.61 μM and linear range from 40–250 μM to 1–500 μM, after optimization of conditions. Evaluation of obtained results by proposed method, was done using atomic absorption spectroscopy. The excellent precision and accuracy of achievement results, appear that designed nanoprobe is a promising novel method for ferric ion detection in biological systems.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the N/B co-doped CDs (Inset: N/B co-doped CDs solution images under day-light (left) and ultraviolet-light (right) using a 365 nm UV lamp, (B) Fluorescence spectra of the N/B co-doped CDs (slit ex/em: 15/3) at different excitation wavelength, (C) Excitation (at 360 nm) and emission (at 440 nm) spectra of N/B co-doped CDs solution.
XPS spectra of the of B/N co-doped CDs (A) high resolution B1S (B), C1S (C), N1S (D), and O1S (E) peaks of the B/N co-doped CDs.

(A) pH effect on the fluorescence intensity of N/B co-doped CDs from pH 2–11 using Tris-buffer, (B) Ionic strength effect (KCl concentration) on the fluorescence intensity of N/B co-doped CDs, (C) Stepwise various of fluorescence intensity of carbon dots by adjusting of conditions including EDTA, KCl and Tris-buffer (pH 5) (D) Representation of quenching fluorescence of N/B co-doped CDs (with λex = 360 nm) after adding blood serum (500 μL) at different time intervals (2, 5, 10, 15, 30 min), “Blank” shows the fluorescence intensity of N/B doped CDs before the addition of blood serum (optimization of incubation time for completion of ferric ions release of serum transferrin).

(A) Ion selective investigation of the N/B co-doped CDs in presence of EDTA as a masking agent. The red bars expose the fluorescence quenching intensity of N/B co-doped CDs in the presence of presented metal ions, while the blue bars represent the N/B co-doped CDs fluorescence which are quenched under metal ions, and EDTA addition, (B) The red bars show fluorescence intensity of N/B co-doped CDs in the presence of EDTA and metal ions, while the blue bars are related to fluorescence quenching of N/B co-doped CDs in presence of metal ions, EDTA and Fe3+ (the final concentration of EDTA of metal ions were 0.5 and 2 mM, respectively), (C) Influence of some biological compounds on the N/B co-doped CDs fluorescence intensity with a final concentration of 2 mM (D) UV–vis absorption spectrums for N/B co-doped CDs, FeCl3 and mixing of N/B co-doped CDs and FeCl3.
(A) Fluorescence quenching (F0 – F) as a function of the various ferric ion concentration at λex = 360 nm, (B) Linear range of calibration curve in the range of 1 to 500 µM with R2 = 0.9976.

Standard addition method for Fe(III) detection of serum sample (A) Gradual reduction of intensity of N/B co-doped CDs fluorescence peaks with increasing concentration of Fe(III) standard solution, (B) The graph of standard addition by drawing (F0 – F) versus volume of adding Fe(III) standard solution (1 mM).
Analysis of ferric ions in serum sample by three assays (n = 3).
Comparison of the proposed probe with other probes for ferric ions determination by fluorimetric technique.

head.ce:title: In-situ determination of ferric ions in human blood serum using highly fluorescence nitrogen and boron co-doped carbon dots nanoprobe without serum preparation step: A direct and solvent-free method

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Kashan, Kashan, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Kashan, Kashan, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Different methods have been reported for Fe3+ ions determination as an essential element in many physiological processes, but most of these methods suffer from the consumption of solvent and time in the blood serum sample preparation stage. In this study, we report fabrication of a selective and sensitive high fluorescent nanoprobe based on the boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots for Fe3+ determination in aqueous solution and human blood serum as an important biological sample. Interestingly, under mildly acidic conditions, adjusted ionic strength, and the existence of EDTA, Fe3+ ions can be released from human serum transferrin, with no need for serum protein removal process as a separate step in sample preparation. Indeed, based on the innovative approach, both steps (consisting of Fe3+ ion release of protein and its detection) are in-situ performed. Notably, limit of detection of Fe3+ was improved from 4.72 μM to 0.61 μM and linear range from 40–250 μM to 1–500 μM, after optimization of conditions. Evaluation of obtained results by proposed method, was done using atomic absorption spectroscopy. The excellent precision and accuracy of achievement results, appear that designed nanoprobe is a promising novel method for ferric ion detection in biological systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, carbon dots as promising fluorescent materials have attracted tremendous attention, owing to their attractive properties including excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, multiple functional groups, excitation-dependent emission and high resistance to photobleaching [1–4]. Therefore, carbon dots have been widely applied in bioimaging [5,6], optoelectronic devices [7,8], fluorescence sensors [9,10], drug delivery [11,12], nanomedicine [13–15], catalyst [16,17]. Also, these nanomaterials have been applied in making fluorescence-based sensors and biosensors design to measure metal ions or various biomolecules in biological fluids (including blood, urine, cerebrospinal), such as Fe3+ [6,18–20], Hg2+ [21,22], Cr6+ [23], Cu2+ [24], Pb2+ [25,26], Zn2+ [27], glucose [28,29], cholesterol [30], cysteine [31], ascorbic acid [32], hemoglobin [33], dopamine [34], histidine [35], creatinine [36], immunoglobulin G [37], and coanzyme A [38]. Among of pivotal elements for living organisms, ferric ion is one of the most essential metal ions in biological systems and physiological and pathological processes such as enzyme catalysis, oxygen transport, RNA synthesis and cellular metabolism [39]. Therefore, the precise measuring of Fe3+ concentration level and its control in waters for environmental safety is necessary. Besides, the decreased level of Fe3+ in human body leads to different diseases like anemia and cirrhosis [40–42], that there are too few red blood cells, in these conditions [43], whereas, the decreased level of Fe3+ in the blood causes several diseases like Alzheimer,s, aging disease, liver damage and kidney failure [39]. More importantly, the abnormal ferric ion level in the blood can be displayed cancer or organ dysfunction [44]. The well-merit total content of ferric ions is about 4 g in the human body and 5.357 μΜ in drinking water, according to Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) [45]. In other words, iron overload and deficiency can both impair cellular functions. So far, several methods based on spectrometric techniques have been reported for Fe3+ detection and quantification such as atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), atomic emission spectroscopy (AES), inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [46]. In contrast to the above-mentioned techniques that have required expensive and complex devices, complex sample preparation, time-consuming and sophisticated instrumentations, fluorescence-based sensing has significant advantages of high rapidity, sensitivity, selectivity and simplicity [7]. Thus, researchers continue to improve selectivity, detection limit, reproducibility and sensitivity of sensors for increasing their performance with the rapid development of nanotechnology [9]. In many previous studies, the blood serum was deproteinized using ethanol, acetonitrile or trichloroacetic acid as an extracting agent to iron release from transferrin protein [39,47,48]. Human serum transferrin (hTf) is a single-chain glycoprotein containing amino acids with molecular masses of approximately 80 kDa [49] that transports iron in the blood-circulatory system [50] and its levels are affected iron deficiency anemia [51] or iron overload disease [52]. Due to the indispensable role of transferrin as a potential transporter of iron in the blood and regulator of iron metabolism, it has attracted much attention to study the mechanism of iron uptake and release from the Fe-Transferrin complex [53]. Also, dependence of iron release rate on pH and ionic strength (anion chloride concentration) was investigated. Due to the existence of an N- and C-terminal domain, transferrin can bind by each of these lobes to one iron atom and consequently, human serum diferric transferrin (hFe2Tf) formation. Therefore, transferrin is known as an iron-binding protein or iron transport, so that can be bonded to Fe3+ with unusually great affinity [54,55]. In another study, He and co-workers [56] reported that iron release from the N-lobe of transferrin at mildly acidic pH, is related to the anion bonding ability to the protein, according to competition reaction between ferric ion and chloride ion.

In the present study, at first, we synthesized N/B co-doped CDs using citric acid (as carbon source) and ethylenediamine / boric acid as N/B co-doped with high quantum yield by one-step hydrothermal method. In the following, the fluorescence quenching mechanism for N/B co-doped CQDs-Fe3+ system was explored. Then, we developed a selective and sensitive fluorescence platform for Fe3+ detection and determination based on fluorescence quenching of N/B co-doped CDs in the presence of EDTA (as a masking agent), so that, among all of the metal ions, any ferric ion can cause fluorescence quenching of the carbon dots, selectively. In the final step, N/B co-doped CDs were successfully applied for Fe3+ determination in human blood serum as the real sample, with no required deprotinizing step of blood serum. Simplification of measurement steps is due to the iron release of transferrin in optimized conditions (pH, ionic strength, EDTA and incubation time) and in-situ ferric ion detection via N/B co-doped CDs fluorescence quenching. For evaluation of the result's accuracy and validation of the proposed sensor, we analyzed the human serum sample of a healthy volunteer by two methods, including atomic absorption spectroscopy (as a reference method) and colorimetric method (as a routine procedure in a medical laboratory).
Relevant information is provided in the Supplementary Material.

At first, N/B co-doped CDs were prepared by one-step hydrothermal method with slight changes [57], (details of the synthesis are given in the Supplementary Material). Then, we investigated the analytical performance of the proposed nanoprobe according to two methods, including calibration curve and standard addition method In both methods, at first N/B co-doped CDs solution (with 0.1 mg/mL as final concentration) was prepared in the presence of EDTA (0.5 M), KCl (0.2 M) and Tris-buffer (pH = 5, 0.05 M) as the optimal conditions of the reaction medium. After that, the resulting mixed solutions were scrutinized according to the two approaches. (1) Calibration curve method: briefly, different volumes of FeCl3 standard solution (1 mM) were added to a series of volumetric flasks for preparing final concentrations of (0, 2, 6, 10, 20, 50, 75, 100, 150, 200, 300 and 500 μΜ). In the absence of FeCl3, the fluorescence intensity of N/B co-doped CDs corresponds to F0 (as blank) and in the presence of FeCl3, the fluorescence intensity corresponds to F. After optimal incubation period (for ∼ 15 min) at the room temperature, calibration curve was obtained by plotting net fluorescence quenching (ΔF = F0 − F) against of Fe3+ concentration for each solution. Then, unknown concentration of ferric ions in serum sample of a healthy volunteer (500 µL) was calculated using received signal and regression equation and considering 10-fold dilution of the serum sample. (2) Standard addition method: at first, equal volumes (200 μL) of the same human serum as before were transferred to the above-mentioned mixed solution in each volumetric flask. Then, the different volumes of standard solution of FeCl3 were added to each one and the obtained mixed solutions were incubated at the room temperature (∼15 min) for the in situ release of iron ions from transferrin. Eventually, the fluorescence emission spectra of the solutions were recorded at about 440 nm (with excitation wavelength fixed at 360 nm). The standard addition curve was obtained by drawing the net quenching fluorescence (ΔF = F0 – F) versus volume of added standard Fe3+ (1 mM) solution. In quenching fluorescence (ΔF), F0 and F refer to fluorescence intensity of N/B co-doped CDs before and after Fe3+ addition. Then, the unknown iron (ΙΙΙ) content in the serum sample was calculated via the extrapolation method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of synthesized N/B co-doped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To study the absorption properties of N/B co-doped CDs, the absorption wavelength was scanned from 200 to 600 nm (Fig. 1 A). The UV–Vis analysis of N/B co-doped CDs indicated two original peaks at 215 nm due to aromatic π orbitals from the carbon core or π-π* transitions (CC bond), and another peak at 350 nm due to n-π* transitions (CO or CN groups). As a result of trapping excited state energy at the surface states, the latter peak can be commonly attributed to in the highly fluorescent carbon dots. As shown in Fig. 1A, in addition to the absorption peak at 215 nm, another shoulder peak is observed around 255 nm. Typically, these absorption peaks are due to aromatic π system with extended conjugation as in the carbon dots structure. Thus, the shoulder peak around 255 nm can be related to π-π* transitions. With respect to the existence of different groups on carbon dots surface, it can be suggested that each of these functional groups creates an energy level gap between the LUMO and HOMO orbitals. Hence, the n-π* transition mode of the B/N co-doped CDs exhibits an asymmetric absorption peak around 370 nm, in addition to the clear peak at 350 nm [57–59]. The water-soluble synthesized N/B co-doped CDs were brownish and bright blue in color (inset in Fig. 1A) under day-light and UV-light (365 nm), respectively. Also, the photoluminescence emitted spectra (Fig. 1B) of N/B co-doped CDs were recorded at different excitation wavelengths (with 10 nm increment). The photoluminescence of N/B co-doped CDs showed a wavelength-dependent property, so that, maximum fluorescence intensity of N/B co-doped CDs was emitted at 440 nm under an excitation wavelength of 360 nm (Fig. 1C). According to the previous study, the emissive behavior of the carbon quantum dots is immensely dependent on their surface functionalities [60].

The quantum yield (QY ∼ 61%) of N/B co-doped CDs was measured against quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 as a standard, as shown in Fig. S1. The FT-IR spectrum of N/B co-doped CDs demonstrated the presence of functional groups)Fig. S2). TEM image of N/B co-doped CDs nanoparticles is indicated in Fig. S3.

The elemental composition and elemental mapping of the N/B co-doped CDs were determined by XPS (X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy) and EDS (Electron-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy) analysis (see Fig. 2 and Fig. S4). According to results of XPS analysis (see Fig. 2A), four predominant peak (with strong binding energy peaks) were determined in this spectrum with 4.44% of B, 26.94% of C, 6.06% of N, and 62.56% of O, respectively. These results can be confirmed that NH2 and B(OH)2 groups were well transferred into N/B co-doped CDs skelton. The high resolution peak of O1S band was convoluted into three peaks at 531.7, 532.3, and 533.3 eV corresponding to CO, COH/COC and BO, respectively. Also, the high resolution XPS spectrum of C1S exhibited three peaks including CB, CC/CC and CO, corresponding to 283.7, 284.6 and 287.1 eV, respectively. N1S XPS spectrum was decomposed into three peaks including NH, NC, and NB at 400.5, 399.5 and 398.6 eV, respectively. For B1S band, two peaks at 191.7 and 190.9 eV can be attributed to BC, and BO, respectively [57,58]. Also, EDS spectra of N/B co-doped CDs (Fig. S4) demonstrate the existence of C, B, N, and O elements.

Optimizing conditions are important from two aspects: 1) achieving maximum iron release from transferrin; 2) reaching the maximum sensitivity through the high efficiency of fluorescence quenching in the presence of released iron ions. In fact, these two optimized conditions must occur simultaneously and in situ, to perform ferric ion measurement with favorable accuracy and precision. With the knowledge that we have the parameters affecting iron release, the factors such as the pH, ionic strength, chelating agent and incubation time were optimized as follows. Each of the aforementioned parameters can play a dual role in the process of determination of ferric ions in blood serum.

The effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of N/B co-doped CDs, as a sensor was investigated in a separate experiment. Due to the presence of –COOH and –NH2 groups on the N/B co-doped CDs surface, the proposed fluorescence probe was pH-dependent. Intensity variations of fluorescence spectra of N/B co-doped CDs in the presence of Tris-buffer (pH 2 to 11, 0.05 M), were recorded (Fig. 3 A). At pH 5 the fluorescence intensity of N/B co-doped CDs was maximum value, but under strongly acidic or basic pH conditions was weak. Based on some previous studies [19], photoinduced electron transfer (PET) can usually occur through free lone pair electrons of pyridinic nitrogen in nitrogen-doped carbon dots. Also, due to the interaction between lone pair electrons of nitrogen in amine groups on the N/B co-doped CDs with protons, a weak fluorescence was seen at acidic pH. Herein, we believe that increase or decrease of pH would lead to protonation-deprotonation of carboxylic acid/hydroxyl groups, COOH/OH and boric acid groups, –B(OH)2 (contain B atom with empty orbital), and as a result, alteration of charge is take placed on the carbon dots surface. From another point of view, based on previous studies, the rate of iron release from Fe-Transferrin reaches its maximum value drastically on lowering the pH [61]. In this study, Baldwin and co-workers graphically indicated that at a constant acidic pH (∼4.5), the iron release rate constantly increases to some extent by increasing EDTA concentration up to 50 mM. In contrast, concerning this investigation, can be resulted that the rate constant value is decreased by increasing of pH value. Also, based on studies conducted by Zak and co-worker [62] using a spectrofluorometric method, the iron release rate from the transferrin was accelerated at low pH (∼5.5). On the other hand, the overall charge on the protein will become more positive at lower pH, so, these conditions can favor the approach of negatively charged ligands (such as EDTA) to transferrin.

According to the previous studies [63], the exact effect of anion on iron release is a controversial subject, but it is clear that chloride accelerates iron release at acidic pH and in contrast, it retards iron release at neutral pH. Also, the experimental study of the chloride effect on iron release at three different pH values was conducted by He and co-workers [63]. Based on the results of their research, the rate constant maximum (K, min−1) was obtained at pH ≈ 5.5 and [KCl] ≈ 0.2 M. Our investigations show that the ionic strength can act in two ways: as the first aspect, the amount of ionic strength did not greatly affect the fluorescence intensity of N/B co-doped CDs at different KCl concentrations, therefore, this negligible effect is considered as an advantage for us. Even in concentrations of KCl up to 2.4 M the fluorescence intensity of N/B co-doped CDs only slightly changed (Fig. 3B). Consequently, N/B co-doped CDs can have great potential for sensing applications in physiological conditions. As the second aspect, it has been known that ionic strength can function as an effective parameter on the rate of iron release from monoferric transferrin, based on the previous studies [64]. According to these investigations, it can be concluded that the presence of salts (according to the relevant kinetic equations) can cause changes in the reaction kinetics. Alternatively, among the anions, chloride can in particular accelerate the kinetics of iron release from transferrin, by binding to it [64]. Also, the presence of anion is a principal prerequisite for iron removal from transferrin, so a linear relationship between iron release rate and chloride concentration ([Cl-]) is observed. According to the above discussion, the final concentration of KCl was adjusted equal to 0.2 M in the reaction medium. Herein, although ionic strength has a negligible effect on the N/B co-doped CDs fluorescence intensity, but it can both increase the rate of iron release from the transferrin and fluorescence quenching in the presence of ferric ions. As presented in Fig. 3C, maximum fluorescence quenching occurs in the presence of KCl and response time was decreased because of increasing reaction kinetic.

Serum transferrin and other proteins of the transferrin family with very high affinity bind to two high-spin Fe3+ ions [65]. Hence, iron strong sequestering agents, such as EDTA (a strong chelating agent) not only can remove iron from transferrin, but also can prevent the reduction of carbon dot fluorescence intensity against metal ions (except ferric ion) as a masking agent, because, EDTA is worked as the main barrier against fluorescence quenching of carbon dots against metal ions. Likewise, EDTA can prevent rebinding of released iron to transferrin.

Based on the previous studies, at pH ∼ 5.5, the release of iron from the N-lobe was very fast, so it was completed after two minutes, while the rate of iron release from the C-lobe is slower than the release rate from the N-lobe [66]. In a typical experiment, the effect of incubation time on iron release from the human serum transferrin was investigated under optimal conditions. As shown in Fig. 3(D), by addition of 500 μL of blood serum to the mixture of N/B co-doped CDs (0.1 mg/mL), Tris-buffer (pH 5, 0.05 M), KCl (0.2 M) and EDTA (0.5 M) and dilution of the mixed solution up to 5 mL using deionized water, the fluorescence quenching of N/B co-doped CDs was recorded at different time intervals (including 2, 5, 10, 15 and 30 min). Collectively, these results indicate that about 15 min as incubation time is a sufficient and proper time for completion of iron release from the transferrin and fluorescence quenching of N/B co-doped CDs process. In fact, after 15 min incubation time, the fluorescence quenching intensity remained constant. Therefore, the mixed solutions were incubated during this time interval before each fluorescence measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of optimized factors on the quenching efficiency of N/B co-doped CDs in the presence of ferric ions in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: By adjusting the above-mentioned optimal conditions, we recorded the fluorescence intensity of N/B co-doped CDs, with and without Fe3+ addition. Indeed, under optimal conditions, fluorescence intensity and fluorescence quenching of N/B co-doped CDs reached their maximum value in the absence and the presence of ferric ions, respectively. Fig. 3C shows the effect of each of the effective parameters on fluorescence quenching of N/B co-doped CDs in a typical experiment, so that the fluorescence quenching intensity reached its maximum value in the presence of iron ions (25 mM), Tris-buffer (pH 5, 0.05 M), KCl (0.2 M) and EDTA (0.5 M). Hence, we performed calibration curve method before and after setting the optimal conditions with the improvement of LOD from 4.7 µM to 0.61 µM, respectively. Also, the analysis of human blood serum as a real sample using two methods (calibration curve and standard addition) was performed under setting optimal conditions. Based on the aforementioned phenomena, we designed a rapid, simple, direct and solvent-free method for the quantitative determination of iron ions in human blood serum.

Selectivity is one of the most important parameters for each sensing system [28], thus, the performance of the proposed nanoprobe was evaluated in this study. Also, the complexity of real samples indicates a great challenge to the carbon dots for metal ion detection, so this subject can be affected not only in selectivity but also in sensitivity. Briefly, the proposed sensor response is based on the fluorescence quenching of N/B co-doped CDs in presence of Fe3+ ions. Therefore, selectivity study was carried out in the absence (as control) and the presence of different metal ions like K+, Na+, Ag+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Fe3+, and coexisting biomolecules like L-cysteine, lactic acid, melamine, urea, uric acid, glucose, dopamine, ascorbic acid and creatinine. To prevent hydroxide precipitation of metal ions, the pH value of the solutions was adjusted below 7 before performing selectivity tests. After incubation for about 5 min by manual shaking, the fluorescence spectra were recorded under λex = 360 nm. Fig. 4(A-C) shows the effect of various ions and biological molecules on the fluorescence emission of N/B co-doped CDs under the same conditions. The fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) under ions addition was investigated in the absence and presence of EDTA. According to Fig. 4A, the red and blue bars show the fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) of N/B co-doped CDs (0.1 mg/mL) in the presence of various metal ions (2 mM) before and after of EDTA addition. Except for Fe3+, several metal ions (including Na+, Ag+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Zn2+ and Hg2+) can cause the fluorescence quenching of the N/B co-doped CDs, in the absence of EDTA. This event happened due to nonspecific interaction between functional groups of the carbon dots surface and metal ions [1], but, after adding EDTA, the metal ions were inactivated for interaction with N/B co-doped CDs. In these conditions, a competitive mixture is formed and fluorescence is recovered. The blue bars in Fig. 4A are related to recovered fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) of N/B co-doped CDs solution (0.1 mg/mL) and metal ions (2 mM), with approximate values after adding EDTA (0.5 M) to the solution. According to the results, (F/F0) values were increased for all ions, except for Fe3+ ion. Therefore, the selectivity test indicates that for the Fe3+ detection, most metal ions in the presence of EDTA as a masking agent, can coexist in the solution without any interference. In general, based on the selectivity test, N/B co-doped CDs can be deemed as a selective ferric ion sensor. In the next step, as seen in Fig. 4B, a constant concentration of each metal ion solution (2 mM) was added to the mixed solution consisting of N/B co-doped CDs and EDTA in the presence (blue bars) and absence (red bars) of Fe3+ (2 mM). Obviously, the blue bars indicate inability of EDTA to prevent fluorescence quenching of N/B co-doped CDs in the presence of Fe3+ ions with other metal ions. The red bars are depicted the fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) of N/B co-doped CDs (0.1 mg/mL) in the presence of EDTA (0.5 M) and the metal ions (2 mM). At each stage, the solutions were incubated for ∼ 5 min for the reaction completed by manual shaking. It is clear that N/B co-doped CDs fluorescence intensity almost remains unchanged or with a slight decrease in fluorescence intensity compared to the control (blank) in these conditions. Based on this assay, EDTA as a masking agent could be chelated to above–mentioned metal ions, except for Fe3+ ions, due to the strong binding affinity of Fe3+ relative to amine groups on the carbon dots surface [67]. To explain the further electrophilic ability of metal ions, two parameters play an essential role in electronic affinity, including standard electrode potential (E0) and charge density. Among metal ions which applied in the selectivity test, Al3+ and Fe3+ have charge densities much higher than other metal ions, but E0 for Fe3+ is much higher than Al3+, therefore, Fe3+ has strongest electrophilic ability than the other metal ions [68]. According to Fig. 4 C, the fluorescence quenching effect of some biomolecules on the N/B co-doped CDs was greatly negligible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and photostability studies of N/B co-doped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Relevant information is provided in the Supplementary Material (Fig. S5 and Fig. S6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Studying the interaction between ferric ions and N/B co-doped CDs (calculation of K-value)
ce:section.ce:para:
In general, fluorescence quenching of carbon dots can be a result of two mechanisms including static or dynamic quenching [67]. For investigation of fluorescence quenching process, we calculated the binding constant (K) to assess the binding affinity between the fluorescent molecule (N/B co-doped CDs) and quencher (Fe3+) for both states (with and without optimization conditions) by Lineweaver-Burk equation [19].

where F0 and F are fluorescence intensity of the N/B co-doped CDs in the absence and the presence of Fe3+ as a quencher, respectively and [Q] is the concentration of quencher (Fe3+). After adding 100 μL of Fe3+ (0.1 M) solution to 100 μL of N/B co-doped CDs (5 mg/mL) and dilution up to a volume of 5 mL, fluorescence intensity was recorded at 360 nm as excitation wavelength (Fig. S7). Then, the K-value was calculated through equation (1) for both states: (1) without optimization of conditions, that is 9.25 × 103 M−1 (according to Fig. S7A) and (2) after optimization conditions, that is obtained 6.21 × 104 M−1 (according to Fig. S7B). In fact, after improving the conditions in the sensing system, the Fe3+ ions can bind more tightly to the functional groups (specifically –NH2 groups) on the surface of carbon dots. Therefore, it can be understood that quenching process originates from static quenching due to non-fluorescent complex formation by interaction of carbon dots (as fluorophore) at the ground state with Fe3+ ions (as quencher). Thus, changes in the absorption spectra (Fig. 4D) of carbon dots due to their interaction with ferric ions should be observed [69]. According to the above discussion, it can be concluded that, the static quenching mechanism can be proposed in the desired system as a possible mechanism. The formation of a stable metal complex is attributed to a strong chemical bonding between Fe3+ and functional groups of carbon dots. Likewise, the ferric ions can interact with the amine groups on the CDs surface and as a result, electrons (at excited state) transfer to the half-filled 3d orbital of ferric ions [70]. After light absorption through the formed complex, these return to the ground state immediately, without photon emission. It should be noted, by addition of EDTA to the CDs/Fe3+ system, due to the strong coordination capability between Fe3+ and EDTA, part of ferric ions released from COOH on the carbon dots [1]. However, in the present study, the fluorescence intensity of N/B co-doped CDs/Fe3+ system was almost unchanged, after addition of EDTA. Therefore, we conclude that COOH groups did not participate in the formation of the bonds with Fe3+ ions, but NH2 groups bind to ferric ions via lone pair electrons of nitrogen atoms. Therefore, N/B co-doped CDs can be investigated as a fluorescence-based nanoplatform for the detection of ferric ions via a fluorescence “turn-off” process. The good detection performance of this nanoprobe may be due to the high affinity N/B co-doped CDs toward Fe(III).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of designed sensor for Fe3+ ion assay by calibration curve method
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, calibration curve was achieved by plotting (F0 – F) as signal response versus ferric ion concentration (at λex = 360 nm) according to optimized conditions, as shown in Fig. 5 and in non-optimized conditions (see Fig. S8). In the first assay (under optimized conditions), various concentrations of FeCl3 standard solution (1, 10, 30, 70, 100, 150, 200, 300, 400 and 500 μΜ) were added in a series of 5 mL of volumetric flask containing 100 µL of N/B co-doped CDs (5 mg/mL). After adding of EDTA (0.5 M) to this mixed solution, Tris-buffer (pH 5, 0.05 M) and KCl (0.2 M) were added to all of the volumetric flasks, too. The regression equation was (F0 - F) = 101.67 [Fe3+] + 5647.9 with R2 = 0.9976 and a linear range of 1 – 500 µM with limit of detection (LOD) of 0.61 µM. In this equation, F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of N/B co-doped CDs in the absence and the presence of the ferric ions, respectively. For determination of ferric ion concentration in real sample by the proposed sensor, 500 µL of homogenized healthy volunteer blood serum as the real sample was added directly to the 2.6 mL of mixed solution (including EDTA (0.5 M), KCl (0.2 M), Tris-buffer (0.05 M) and N/B co-doped CDs (0.1 mg/mL)). Then, the mixed solution was diluted to the final volume of 5 mL using ultrapure water and the fluorescence quenching was obtained as the response signal after incubation time (∼15 min). The ferric ion concentration was calculated by the regression equation and considering the dilution factor (10-fold), too. The average concentration of ferric ions in the serum sample was found to be 1.090 mg L–1 (with 0.459% as RSD).

As an additional study, under non-optimized conditions, the ferric ion concentration in the serum sample was calculated to equal 0.987 mg L–1 using (F0 – F) = 80.752 [Fe3+] + 2507 regression equation with R2 = 0.9901, after dilution of the final mixture up to 10–fold.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of designed sensor for Fe3+ ion assay in the real sample by standard addition method
ce:section.ce:para: Concerning to obtained results, it can be found that the proposed nanoprobe was able to determine ferric ions content, with no requirement for serum protein removal in compared with other ferric ions sensors. The results and uncertainty of serum iron measurement using the proposed method (based on the calibration curve method and the standard addition method) were consistent with the obtained results from the reference method. Eventually, the performance of the proposed sensor for ferric ions (as analyte) was compared with previous reported sensors (Table 2 ). It is obvious that linear range for the proposed nanoprobe is significantly improved compared to other methods.

In summary, we synthesized highly fluorescence carbon dots as nanoprobes and developed a simple, rapid, solvent-free and one-step selective sensing method to determine the iron ion concentration in human blood serum. By using the proposed method, the serum iron ions were measured without adding protein removal reagents as serum pretreatment. By comparing the results obtained from the proposed method with the reference AAS method, it can be understood that the proposed nanoprobe is a new promising nanoplatform for direct detection of ferric ions in human serum with at least RSD and acceptable responses. Consequently, no need to use organic solvents, reduction of analysis time and reduction of the average uncertainty of the results can be mentioned as the advantages of designed nanoprobe.
Seyed Mostafa Jafari: Methodology, Investigation. Saeed Masoum: Supervision, Project administration.
